buggy_code:
import pandas as pd

# Long-format DataFrame with asset returns
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'date': ['2020-01-01', '2020-01-01', '2020-01-02', '2020-01-02', '2020-01-03'],
    'asset': ['AAPL', 'MSFT', 'AAPL', 'MSFT', 'AAPL'],
    'return': [0.01, 0.02, -0.01, None, 0.03]
})

# Pivot to wide format for factor model
wide = df.pivot_table(index='date', columns='asset', values='return')

# Drop any rows with NaN to get clean data
wide = wide.dropna()

# Compute correlation matrix for portfolio optimization
corr = wide.corr()
clean_code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'date': ['2020-01-01', '2020-01-01', '2020-01-02', '2020-01-02', '2020-01-03'],
    'asset': ['AAPL', 'MSFT', 'AAPL', 'MSFT', 'AAPL'],
    'return': [0.01, 0.02, -0.01, None, 0.03]
})

wide = df.pivot_table(index='date', columns='asset', values='return')

# Check how much data is dropped BEFORE dropping
print(f'Rows before dropna: {len(wide)}')
print(f'NaN counts:\n{wide.isna().sum()}')

# Use pairwise correlation instead of dropping rows
# This uses all available data for each pair
corr = wide.corr(min_periods=20)

# Or only drop if ALL assets are missing that day
wide_clean = wide.dropna(how='all')
description: dropna() silently removes entire dates when any single asset has a missing return.
explanation: After pivoting to wide format, a single missing return for one asset on one date causes dropna() to remove that entire date for ALL assets. In a 500-asset universe, if any one asset is missing data on a given day, you lose that day's returns for all 499 other assets too. Over a multi-year backtest this can silently remove 30-40% of your data, biasing the correlation matrix and portfolio optimization toward periods when all assets happened to trade normally.
suggested_fix: Use corr(min_periods=N) which computes pairwise correlations using all available data. Or use dropna(how='all') to only drop dates where every asset is missing.

issues.title: dropna() after pivot silently removes valid data for all assets
